critique.summary: The article chronicles the development of the world's first electric grid in London, detailing the early fragmented electricity supply systems, the challenges faced during the First World War, and the eventual creation of a national grid. It highlights key technological and political milestones, including the transition from direct current to alternating current, the establishment of the Central Electricity Board, and the post-war nationalization and later privatization of the electricity network.
mainPoints: In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, London's electricity supply was fragmented with many small, incompatible systems using different voltages and frequencies.

Alternating current (AC) technology became the backbone of modern electricity systems due to its efficiency in long-distance transmission, despite initial resistance from proponents of direct current (DC).
The First World War exposed the inefficiencies of the fragmented system, leading to government encouragement of interconnection and moves toward a coordinated national grid.
The Electricity (Supply) Act 1919 created regional joint electricity authorities, but without enforcement powers, limiting progress.
The Central Electricity Board, established by the Electricity Supply Act 1926, created a synchronized AC grid starting operations in 1933, known as the National Grid.
Post-World War II nationalization under the Electricity Act 1947 consolidated generation and transmission, improving coordination and investment.
The Supergrid was built between 1950 and 1960 to connect regional grids at higher voltages, enhancing capacity and efficiency.
Privatization in the late 20th century reintroduced private ownership and competition, with mixed outcomes on investment and pricing.
In 2024, the UK government re-nationalized the operation of the electricity network, moving towards national operation with private ownership.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a historically accurate and well-researched account of the development of the UK's electricity grid, corroborated by multiple historical sources and widely accepted facts about the evolution of electrical infrastructure. It correctly describes the technological transition from DC to AC, the legislative milestones, and the economic and political factors influencing the grid's development. The details about the Central Electricity Board, the National Grid, and subsequent nationalization and privatization align with established historical records. The article also appropriately contextualizes the challenges and innovations within broader global developments in electricity supply.
considerations: The article focuses primarily on the UK context and does not deeply explore parallel developments in other countries beyond brief mentions, which could provide a more comparative perspective.
It does not extensively discuss the environmental impacts of coal-based electricity generation during the grid's expansion or the transition to renewable energy sources in recent decades.
The social and economic implications of electricity access disparities during the early grid development are not deeply examined.
The article briefly mentions controversies around privatization but does not delve into detailed economic analyses or alternative models for electricity market regulation.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around historical claims about the first electric grids and technical considerations for modern grid improvements, including switching from AC to DC transmission. Some comments provide historical context, while others discuss technical challenges and future grid resilience.

comments.author: cogman10
comments.text:
If we could change grids in one way, the best thing we could probably do is switch from HVAC for transmission to HVDC.

I think the ideal grid would switch from DC to AC either at a substation at central location for a community.

Why might someone do this?

One of the hardest problems to work through is a grid cold start. When a grid goes completely down it takes a monumental effort to bring it back up again. There's a delicate balance that has to be struck with load and other generators coming online. It's hard to do. The AC waveform is a finicky thing that gets pulled and mutilated by every motor or vacuum cleaner that starts running.

With a bunch of AC microgrids joined by a DC major grid, you can completely sidestep that problem. It suddenly becomes just a lot easier to ramp up power production because the deformations to the waveform happen in small local regions, not everywhere in the grid. And further, the other plants just have to watch the DC voltage, they don't need a whole bunch of equipment around syncing with the AC waveform of the grid as a whole.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a technically sound and insightful explanation of grid challenges and potential improvements, reflecting current engineering discussions about HVDC benefits.

comments.author: floathub
comments.text: The power generated from Niagara river stations was traveling on an international "grid" between Canada and the US in the late 1890s.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately references the early international power transmission between Canada and the US, which is well-documented historically.

comments.author: detourdog
comments.text:
I think Great Barrington, MA. might also be able to claim the title of first. It seems to predate this system by a year.

https://www.edisontechcenter.org/GreatBarrington.html
comments.analysis: This comment adds historical nuance by suggesting an alternative claim to the first electric grid, supported by a referenced source.

comments.author: culi
comments.text:
Interestingly enough the Kingdom of Hawaii actually beat this. They already had electric street lights by 1881 on Maui.

Hawaii has a fascinating history being the first indigenous nation recognized by Western nations (until ofc it was illegally annexed by the US to use as a base during the Spanish War). They went from being one of the most technologically advanced nations to now having 50% of homeless people in Hawaii being native Hawaiians after having their land stolen from them and forced into indentured servitude on plantations
comments.analysis: While historically interesting and largely accurate about Hawaii's early electrification and complex history, the comment mixes technical and sociopolitical topics, which are somewhat tangential to the main discussion.

comments.author: gotwaz
comments.text: Fragmentation is natural. The human body doesnt need all this infra and a grid operator/energy schedules to run. Cells dont bill each other if they over/under generate ATP molecules. There is no deviation settlement mech and no need for everything to  sync to some arbit frequency. Watch how the grid changes post Ukraine drone wars where taking out generators, transmission lines and substations has become a fully automated process.
comments.analysis: This comment uses metaphorical language to discuss grid fragmentation and resilience, referencing recent conflict impacts. While insightful, it is less focused and mixes analogies with real-world events.
